episodeOutline.title: The First Bell
episodeOutline.synopsis: It's move-in day at Ridgeline Academy, and player has exactly one day to figure out who they're going to be here. Between a hallway collision with the school's magnetic golden-child Jordan, a lunchroom seating decision, a group project pairing with awkward outsider Ben, and a public humiliation moment that tests everyone's silence, player's small choices quietly build toward one of three very different versions of 'fitting in.'

scenes.title: The Front Steps
scenes.description: Player stands outside the school for the first time, schedule crumpled in hand, watching streams of students who all seem to already know each other. An inner monologue establishes the stakes: this is a fresh start, and nobody here knows the old version of you.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Anxious anticipation - the blank page feeling

scenes.title: Hallway Collision
scenes.description: Jordan, clearly a well-liked upperclassman, nearly knocks player's books to the floor while walking backward mid-conversation with friends. Jordan turns, notices the new face, and grins - a small crowd's attention flickers toward player for one unscripted second.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Sudden spotlight - the first-impression jolt

scenes.title: Holding Your Ground
scenes.description: Jordan seems genuinely impressed by player's easy comeback and introduces themselves properly, mentioning they'll 'see them around.' A few onlookers register player as someone worth knowing.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Small validation, cautious pride

scenes.title: Quiet Recovery
scenes.description: Player gathers the dropped books without fuss. Jordan barely notices, already turning back to friends, but a passing girl with paint-stained fingers - Maya - gives player a small, knowing half-smile before disappearing into the crowd.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Overlooked but seen by the right person

scenes.title: Trading Jokes
scenes.description: Player volleys the joke right back at Jordan, who laughs out loud and calls player 'quick' in front of friends. It gets a laugh from the group, though one boy at the edge of the circle doesn't quite laugh along.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Adrenaline of a risk that paid off

scenes.title: The Cafeteria Map
scenes.description: Tray in hand, player scans the room: Jordan's table is loud and central, waving player over. Maya sits alone near the windows, sketchbook open, clearly used to eating solo. A handful of open seats sit scattered between groups. The whole rest of the year could hinge on this one choice.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The unspoken social math of a first lunch

scenes.title: Sketchbook Seat
scenes.description: Maya is startled, then quietly pleased, when player sits down. She doesn't say much at first, but shows player a sketch of the school mascot drawn as something absurd, and a real laugh breaks the ice.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Unexpected warmth in an unlikely place

scenes.title: Center Table
scenes.description: Jordan's friends fold player into the conversation easily, but it's fast-moving and full of inside jokes. Being included feels good, but player also notices Maya, alone by the window, glancing over for half a second.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The comfort of the spotlight, tinged with a flicker of guilt

scenes.title: Table Hopping
scenes.description: Player says a quick hello at Jordan's table, then swings by Maya's to compliment her sketchbook before finally settling somewhere in between. It's a little exhausting, but by the end of lunch, three different tables know player's name.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Exhilarating but precarious - stretched in every direction

scenes.title: Partner Assignments
scenes.description: Ms. Alvarez pairs the class for a semester-long project. Player is paired with Ben, a nervous, stammering kid who immediately apologizes for 'probably ruining the grade.' From across the room, Jordan stage-whispers something that gets a laugh at Ben's expense.
scenes.emotionalBeat: A small cruelty in the open, and a choice about complicity

scenes.title: Standing Beside Ben
scenes.description: Player says, loud enough for the room to hear, that they're glad to be paired with Ben. Ben's shoulders drop half an inch in relief. Jordan raises an eyebrow but doesn't push further.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Quiet courage, and a new ally gained

scenes.title: Working It Out Privately
scenes.description: Player doesn't call out Jordan's comment, but leans over to Ben afterward and starts brainstorming project ideas one-on-one. Ben visibly relaxes once the attention is off him.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Support without spectacle

scenes.title: Going Along With It
scenes.description: Player laughs along with Jordan's joke. It feels easy in the moment - until Ben glances over, and player catches the exact second his face falls.
scenes.emotionalBeat: A cheap laugh that curdles almost immediately

scenes.title: Called On
scenes.description: Ms. Alvarez asks Ben to share the group's project idea aloud. He stumbles over his words, and Jordan's table snickers loudly enough for the whole room to hear. Ben goes red and stares at his desk. Ms. Alvarez is distracted at the board and doesn't notice.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The moment where silence becomes a choice too

scenes.title: Out Loud
scenes.description: Player speaks up, calmly backing up Ben's idea and adding to it. The laughter dies fast. Ms. Alvarez notices this time, and gives player an approving nod. Ben looks at player like something has shifted.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The relief and risk of being seen taking a stand

scenes.title: Looking Away
scenes.description: Player stays quiet, and the moment passes. Ben mumbles through the rest of his sentence alone. Later, packing up, Ben won't quite meet player's eyes.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The uncomfortable weight of an unspoken regret

scenes.title: Changing the Subject
scenes.description: Player cracks a self-deprecating joke about their own project idea, pulling the room's attention away from Ben without calling anyone out directly. The laughter shifts targets, and Ben gets a second to recover.
scenes.emotionalBeat: A sideways kindness - not brave, but not nothing

scenes.title: Last Bell
scenes.description: The day is ending. Jordan's group is heading somewhere and half-invites player along. Maya lingers nearby, sketchbook under her arm, clearly deciding whether to say something. Ben walks past alone, glancing back once. Player has one more choice about how today is going to define tomorrow.
scenes.emotionalBeat: The crossroads moment - who do you want to become here

scenes.title: The Real Thing
scenes.description: Player catches up to Ben and Maya instead, asking Ben if the project idea from class could actually work, and inviting Maya to show her sketchbook properly. It's smaller than Jordan's group, but it feels like something real is starting.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Belonging built on honesty rather than performance

scenes.title: In the Group
scenes.description: Player joins Jordan's group, and it feels good - easy laughter, immediate status. But walking away, player notices Ben and Maya leaving separately, and something about the win feels a little hollow.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Popularity's warmth with a cold undercurrent

scenes.title: Both Worlds
scenes.description: Player tells Jordan's group they'll catch up later, then walks partway with Ben and Maya, trading numbers and half-formed plans. Nothing is fully decided yet, but player feels like they can move between these worlds without losing themselves.
scenes.emotionalBeat: Cautious optimism - the sense of options still open

choicePoints.prompt: Jordan almost knocked your books flying and now the hallway is watching. How do you respond?
choicePoints.context: First social moment of the day, with an audience. There's no wrong answer, but there's no neutral one either.

options.text: Laugh it off and introduce yourself with a steady handshake.
Quietly pick up your books and keep moving.
Fire back a joke of your own.

choicePoints.prompt: Where do you sit for your first lunch?
choicePoints.context: Jordan's table is loud and waving you over. Maya is alone by the window. There are also scattered open seats between groups.

options.text: Sit with Maya, the girl reading alone.
Say a quick hello at both, and land somewhere in between.

choicePoints.prompt: Jordan just made a joke at Ben's expense in front of the whole class. What do you do?
choicePoints.context: You've just been paired with Ben for a semester-long project. Everyone heard the comment, including Ben.

options.text: Say, loud enough for the room, that you're glad to be paired with him.
Don't call it out, but immediately start working with Ben one-on-one.

choicePoints.prompt: Ben is stumbling through his answer and Jordan's table is snickering. Ms. Alvarez hasn't noticed. What do you do?
choicePoints.context: This is the moment that could define how the rest of the year feels for Ben - and for you.

options.text: Speak up and back Ben's idea in front of everyone.
Stay quiet and let the moment pass.
Crack a joke about your own idea to pull attention away from Ben.

choicePoints.prompt: The day is ending. Where do you put your energy?
choicePoints.context: Jordan's group has half-invited you along. Maya is lingering nearby. Ben just walked past alone. Tomorrow starts with whatever you decide right now.

options.text: Catch up with Ben and Maya instead.
Tell Jordan's group you'll catch up later, then walk partway with Ben and Maya.

branches.name: The Authentic Path
branches.description: Player consistently chooses connection over status - sitting with Maya, defending Ben, speaking up when it costs something socially.
branches.outcome: Player ends the day with two genuine, if lower-status, friendships and a growing sense of self-respect. Sets up Maya and Ben as recurring allies in future episodes.

branches.name: The Popularity Path
branches.description: Player consistently prioritizes fitting in with Jordan's group, going along with the crowd even when it means small betrayals of Ben and Maya.
branches.outcome: Player ends the day socially secure and instantly 'in,' but with an unresolved thread of guilt and a distanced relationship with Ben and Maya that will resurface later in the season.

branches.name: The Adaptive Path
branches.description: Player moves fluidly between social worlds, offering quieter, indirect support rather than big public stands, and refusing to fully commit to one group.
branches.outcome: Player ends the day with a foothold in multiple social circles and a flexible, still-forming sense of identity - higher social capital than the authentic path, less risk of guilt than the popularity path, but with an open question of who they really are underneath it.

emotionalArc: Anxious anticipation on the front steps
Sudden social spotlight in the hallway
The quiet math of choosing a lunch table
The charged silence of a public choice point
Reflection and self-definition at day's end

characterArcs.arc: Moves from anxious blank slate to someone with a working answer to 'who am I here' - the specific answer depends entirely on player choices.
Introduced as an easy, magnetic golden-child; subtly revealed across branches as either a genuine ally, a careless bystander, or a low-key gatekeeper depending on how player responds to them.
Starts guarded and self-sufficient after being overlooked; opens up meaningfully only if player extends effort toward her, planting a seed for a larger friendship arc later in the season.
Begins visibly anxious about being an easy target; his trajectory across the episode - defended, quietly supported, or left exposed - sets up his season-long relationship with player.

traitMapping.description: Measured through willingness to take social risk without needing group approval first - introducing yourself, speaking up publicly, standing by an unpopular choice.
Measured through attentiveness to overlooked people and willingness to act on their behalf even at social cost - choosing Maya, defending Ben, refusing to laugh along.
Measured through flexible, situational responses that don't collapse into either rigid withdrawal or total conformity - banter, table-hopping, redirecting tension with humor.

relationshipDynamics.dynamic: A test of whether player can engage with charisma and social power without losing themselves in it. Trends toward genuine mutual respect, distant cordiality, or quiet complicity depending on branch.
A slow-burn potential friendship gated entirely on player noticing and choosing her when it isn't the obvious move.
The episode's emotional core - Ben's sense of safety at this new school is directly shaped by whether player defends, quietly supports, or abandons him in front of others.

replayHooks: Players who chose the popularity path will wonder what Maya's sketchbook joke or Ben's real project idea actually was - only visible on the authentic path.
The three lunchroom scenes (5a/5b/5c) reveal different pieces of Jordan and Maya's personalities not visible in other branches.
Jordan's reaction to being turned down at the end (scene-11a/11c) versus being joined (11b) reveals a hidden layer of insecurity under his confidence, only glimpsed if you don't join his group.
Ben's arc changes meaningfully across all three choice-4 options, encouraging replay just to see how differently he responds to being defended vs. ignored vs. subtly protected.

The 'adaptive' branch is the hardest to fully achieve since it requires consistently choosing the indirect option - players often want to replay to see if they can 'thread the needle' across all five choices.
educationalGoals: Model confidence as steady self-assertion rather than dominance or performance
Build empathy by making perspective-taking (noticing Ben's and Maya's emotional states) a rewarded, natural part of the story rather than an explicit lesson
Illustrate adaptability as flexible authenticity - adjusting behavior to context without abandoning core values
Show that first impressions and social choices have consequences that ripple forward, encouraging reflective decision-making
Normalize the idea that there is no single 'correct' way to belong, while still surfacing the emotional costs of certain choices
conversationStarters: Which lunch table would you actually have sat at on your first day somewhere new?
Was there ever a moment you wished you'd spoken up for someone, like in the classroom scene?
Do you think Jordan is a good person, a bad person, or just someone figuring things out too?
Did your choices feel like 'you,' or were you playing a version of yourself you don't usually show?
What do you think happens to Ben and Maya next, depending on which path you chose?

designNotes: The episode uses a 'braided river' structure: choices diverge into short, distinct scenes but reconverge at fixed story pillars (lunch, project assignment, the classroom incident, end of day) to keep production scope manageable while preserving the feeling of consequence. Trait scoring is deliberately invisible - no option is ever framed as 'the confident choice' in the text; instead, trait deltas are inferred from behavior (speaking up, noticing others, flexing between contexts). The three endings are designed to be roughly equally 'valid' emotionally, avoiding a simple good/bad binary, though the authentic branch is written with slightly warmer emotional payoff to subtly reward genuine connection without punishing players who explore other paths.
uncertainties: Whether the reconvergence points (all branches funneling into scene-4, scene-6, scene-8, scene-10) sufficiently preserve the feeling of divergence, or whether players will feel the branches are cosmetic until the very end.

Whether three roughly-equal endings appropriately reward the 'authentic' branch without implicitly coding the popularity branch as a 'wrong answer,' which could undercut the intended non-judgmental tone.
Whether 13 minutes is sufficient given 21 scenes plus 5 choice points - actual pacing may run long if players linger on the classroom incident, which is the emotional peak.
Whether Jordan reads as a genuine three-dimensional character across all branches or risks feeling like a static 'test' the player passes or fails.
alternativesConsidered: A fully non-converging branch structure (each choice permanently forking the story) was considered but rejected as too costly to produce and likely to dilute the emotional throughline for this first episode.

An alternate 'incident' scene involving a cafeteria food-tray accident was considered instead of the classroom answer-mocking scene, but the classroom version was chosen because it more naturally isolates Ben and creates a clear, publicly-witnessed choice point.

A version with only two endings (authentic vs. popularity) was considered, but a third 'adaptive/balanced' ending was added to avoid forcing a binary moral framing and to better map the ADAPTABILITY trait distinctly from CONFIDENCE and EMPATHY.

Making Jordan secretly cruel throughout was considered but rejected in favor of a more ambiguous portrayal, since a straightforwardly 'bad' popular kid would make the popularity branch feel punitive rather than a genuine alternative choice.
